narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an LSAT score of 35, you've clearly put in the work to build a competitive profile for law school, including your upper-middle family income background and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 41.9%—a threshold that led to the regrettable rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with lower predicted failure odds to ensure strong bar passage rates. The most influential factor pulling in your favor was actually your LSAT score, which stands just below the typical 36.7 average but still strongly signaled your potential to pass the bar. Your undergraduate GPA, nearly matching the dataset average of 3.21, also worked positively to suggest you'd succeed. That said, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3—lower than the average of 5.53—was the top factor pushing the prediction toward failure risk, indicating relatively weaker performance in those later undergrad years compared to peers. Your family's upper-middle income level had a modest negative pull, as higher brackets sometimes correlate with slightly elevated failure risks in the data, though this was less decisive. Being a full-time student and your Year 1 decile of 4 (below the 5.71 average) had smaller positive influences toward passing. Overall, while your test scores and GPA aligned closely with successful applicants, the dip in your later-year school rankings tipped the scales against you relative to typical admits who maintain stronger decile progress. This isn't a reflection of your overall potential as a driven individual, but rather how these metrics stacked up in the model's view of bar exam predictors. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average or strengthening your academic record through a post-bac program or relevant experience that highlights improvement in later performance. You might also explore schools with more holistic reviews or appeal with a personal statement addressing your Year 3 context. We're rooting for you—many students in similar situations turn things around and thrive. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
